items.subtopic: TEMPORARY PACING FOR TRANSIENT SUPPORT | PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION
items.title: Management of Bradycardia: Reversible Causes and Temporary Solutions
items.content: In symptomatic patients with sinus node disease, identifying and addressing reversible causes of bradycardia is crucial. This includes evaluating for acute myocardial infarction, electrolyte imbalances, medications like beta blockers, calcium channel blockers, and digoxin, as well as hypothyroidism. Adjusting or discontinuing these medications can sometimes resolve the issue without needing a pacemaker.
keyPoints: Reversible causes of bradycardia include acute myocardial infarction, electrolyte abnormalities, certain medications (beta blockers, calcium channel blockers, digoxin), and hypothyroidism.
Adjusting or discontinuing medications that cause bradycardia should be attempted before considering a pacemaker.
Bradycardia may resolve after the reversal of ischemia with revascularization.
items.reference: TREATMENT · TEMPORARY PACING FOR TRANSIENT SUPPORT | PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION

items.subtopic: TEMPORARY PACING FOR TRANSIENT SUPPORT | PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION
items.title: Tachy-Brady Syndrome: Addressing Tachycardia to Prevent Bradycardia Events
items.content: In patients with tachy-brady syndrome, controlling the underlying tachycardia (such as atrial fibrillation or other supraventricular tachyarrhythmias) can prevent bradycardia events. This may be achieved through antiarrhythmic drug therapy or catheter ablation. If these methods fail to control arrhythmias, permanent pacing might become necessary.
keyPoints: Tachy-brady syndrome involves episodes of tachycardia followed by bradycardia.
Control of tachycardia through antiarrhythmic drugs or catheter ablation can prevent bradycardia events.
Permanent pacing may be necessary if arrhythmia control is not achieved.
items.reference: TREATMENT · TEMPORARY PACING FOR TRANSIENT SUPPORT | PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION

items.subtopic: TEMPORARY PACING FOR TRANSIENT SUPPORT | PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION
items.title: Bradycardia Post-Cardiac Surgery: Understanding and Managing Sinus Node Dysfunction
items.content: After heart transplantation or cardiac surgery, sinus bradycardia may occur due to factors like cardiac denervation or ischemic injury to the SA node. In heart transplant recipients, a normal resting heart rate is typically 90–110 beats/min. Temporary pacing or pharmacologic support with beta-1 adrenergic agonists may be required until SA nodal recovery.
keyPoints: Heart transplantation and cardiac surgery can lead to sinus bradycardia due to denervation or ischemic injury.
Normal resting heart rate in heart transplant recipients is 90–110 beats/min.
Temporary pacing or beta-1 adrenergic agonists may be needed for SA nodal recovery.

items.explanation: Aspirin is not typically associated with causing bradycardia.

items.explanation: Reversal of ischemia with revascularization can alleviate bradycardia caused by cardiac ischemia or MI.

items.statement: Sinus bradycardia after spinal cord injury is primarily due to increased sympathetic tone.
items.explanation: The mechanism of bradycardia after spinal cord injury is enhanced parasympathetic tone, not sympathetic.

items.assertion: Reversal of sinus node disease is possible in all cases.
items.reason: Ischemia and necrosis of pacemaker cells can cause irreversible sinus node disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because ischemia and necrosis can lead to irreversible sinus node disease.
